coredata.dc:title: Effect of reaction temperature on structure and fluorescence properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yi
Wang, Yaling
Feng, Xiaoting
Zhang, Feng
Yang, Yongzhen
Liu, Xuguang
coredata.dc:description: To investigate the effect of reaction temperature and nitrogen doping on the structure and fluorescence properties of carbon dots (CDs), six kinds of nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) were synthesized at reaction temperatures of 120, 140, 160, 180, 200 and 220°C, separately, by using citric acid as carbon source and ammonia solution as nitrogen source. Nitrogen-free CDs (N-free CDs-180) was also prepared at 180°C by using citric acid as the only carbon source for comparison. Results show that reaction temperature has obvious effect on carbonization degree, quantum yield (QY), ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra but less effect on functional groups, nitrogen doping degree and fluorescence lifetime of NCDs. Compared with N-free CDs-180, NCDs-180 possesses enchanced QY and longer fluorescence lifetime. Doping nitrogen has obvious effect on UV–vis absorption and PL spectra but less effect on particles sizes and carbonization degree. The formation mechanism of NCDs is explored: QY of NCDs depends largely on the number of fluorescent polymer chains (FPC), the competition between FPC formation on the surface of NCDs and carbon core growth leads to the change in number of FPC, and consequently to the NCDs with highest QY at appropriate hydrothermal temperature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-free carbon dots
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Formation mechanism
Citric acid
Ammonia solution
Reaction temperature

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21176169 ), International Science & Technology Cooperation Program of China (Grant No. 2012DFR50460 ), Shanxi Provincial Key Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology (Grant No. 2015013002-10 ) and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (Grant No. 2012-038 ) for finacial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of NCDs-140 (a), NCDs-160 (b), NCDs-180 (c), NCDs-200 (d), NCDs-220 (e) and N-free CDs-180 (f). Inset images are the size distribution histograms.
XRD patterns of NCDs-140, NCDs-160, NCDs-180, NCDs-200, NCDs-220 and N-free CDs-180.
FTIR spectra of NCDs-140, NCDs-160, NCDs-180, NCDs-200, NCDs-220 and N-free CDs-180.
(a) XPS, (b) C1s and (c) O1s spectra of NCDs-160, NCDs-180, NCDs-200, NCDs-220 and N-free CDs-180, (d) N1s spectra of NCDs-160, NCDs-180, NCDs-200 and NCDs-220.

UV–vis absorption (a and b) and PL spectra (c) of NCD aqueous solutions. The insets are absorbance at 335nm and fluorescence intensity at 440nm plotted against hydrothermal temperature, separately (The concentration of NCDs is diluted to 1×10−4 times that of the original solution. The excitation and emission slits are both set at 2nm, λex =360nm). (d) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of N-free-180 aqueous solutions which are not diluted.

PL spectra for the aqueous solutions of NCDs and N-free CD-180 with progressively different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300nm to 500nm with increments of 20nm: NCDs-120 (a), NCDs-140 (b), NCDs-160 (c), NCDs-180 (d), NCDs-200 (e), NCDs-220 (f) and N-free CDs-180 (g). Inset in Fig. 6 shows maximum emission wavelength plotted against excitation wavelength.
Determination by slope method of QY of NCDs synthesized at different temperatures and N-free CDs-180.
Fluorescence decay curves of NCDs synthesized at differeent temperatures and N-free CDs-180.
Photoes of NCDs synthesized at different temperatures and N-free CDs-180.
Element analysis of NCDs prepared at different temperatures and N-free CD-180.
QY of NCDs synthesized at different temperatures and N-free CDs-180.
Bi-exponential fitting parameters of fluorescence decay curves of NCDs synthesized at differeent temperatures and N-free CDs-180.

head.ce:title: Effect of reaction temperature on structure and fluorescence properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology), Ministry of Education, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lyuliang University, Lyuliang 033001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center on Advanced Materials Science and Technology, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center on Advanced Materials Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology), Ministry of Education, Taiyuan 030024, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To investigate the effect of reaction temperature and nitrogen doping on the structure and fluorescence properties of carbon dots (CDs), six kinds of nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) were synthesized at reaction temperatures of 120, 140, 160, 180, 200 and 220°C, separately, by using citric acid as carbon source and ammonia solution as nitrogen source. Nitrogen-free CDs (N-free CDs-180) was also prepared at 180°C by using citric acid as the only carbon source for comparison. Results show that reaction temperature has obvious effect on carbonization degree, quantum yield (QY), ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra but less effect on functional groups, nitrogen doping degree and fluorescence lifetime of NCDs. Compared with N-free CDs-180, NCDs-180 possesses enchanced QY and longer fluorescence lifetime. Doping nitrogen has obvious effect on UV–vis absorption and PL spectra but less effect on particles sizes and carbonization degree. The formation mechanism of NCDs is explored: QY of NCDs depends largely on the number of fluorescent polymer chains (FPC), the competition between FPC formation on the surface of NCDs and carbon core growth leads to the change in number of FPC, and consequently to the NCDs with highest QY at appropriate hydrothermal temperature.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are defined as nanoparticles that are mainly comprised of carbon with a size below 10nm [1]. CDs have drawn a lot of attention because of their unique chemical/electronic/optical properties, biocompatibility and low toxicity [2,3], which make them a great promising material for applications in light-emitting diodes (LEDs), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), sensor and biomedical imaging [2–5]. Synthesis methods for CDs can generally be classified into two main groups: top-down cutting and bottom-up carbonization approaches [1,6]. Compared with top-down routes, the bottom-up routes have obvious advantages in adjusting the composition and physical properties of CDs by appropriate selection of diversified organic precursors and carbonization conditions. Bottom-up strategies including combustion, thermal, hydrothermal or microwave treatments [1]. Among them, hydrothermal synthesis strategy is considered to be a simple, direct and efficient way to obtain CDs [7].

Citric acid is an important starting material for the synthesis of CDs through hydrothermal carbonization because the direct process involved and their carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups facilitate dehydration and carbonization [1,8]. Through doping nitrogen [4], the photoluminescence (PL) intensity and quantum yield (QY) of CDs can be enhanced [1]. In our previous work, a one-step hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and urea precursors was carried out to synthesize high QY (42.2%) nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) with hydrophilic surface groups [8]. However, the relationship between QY and structure of NCDs evolving through reaction temperature is in doubt, which significantly hinders the development and use of CD-based materials with desired PL characteristics. As a result, it is highly significant to clarify the relationship, so as to contribute to uncovering clear chemical structure, providing insight into the understanding of the PL mechanism and fine-tuning optical emissions of CDs. This issue will be applicable for some types of citric acid-based CDs.

In this study, we synthesized 6 kinds of NCDs at different hydrothermal temperatures (120, 140, 160, 180, 200, 220°C) by employing citric acid as carbon source and ammonia solution as nitrogen source, and investigated the effect of reaction temperature on the structure and fluorescence properities of NCDs by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), elemental analysis, PL, ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence lifetime. The evolution of NCDs from polymer-like NCDs to carbogenic NCDs is explored as the reaction progresses from low temperature to high temperature: QY of NCDs largely depends on the number of fluorescent polymer chains (FPC), which is in turn affected by FPC formation rate on the surface of NCDs and carbon core growth rate. On one hand, with the increase of reaction temperature, the number of FPC increases, which leads to increased QY; on the other hand, with the further increase of reaction temperature, carbon core gradually grows up by means of the consumption of FPC, which leads to decreased QY, and the competition result of above two factors is that QY of NCDs reaches its maximun at a suitable temperature. This mechanism builds the relationship among reaction temperature, QY and structures of NCDs. In addition, the effect of nitrogen on structures and fluorescence properties of CDs is also investigated by comparison of NCDs prepared at 180°C (NCDs-180) with nitrogen-free CDs prepared at 180°C (N-free CDs-180) by only using citric acid as carbon source.

Citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O) and ammonia solution (NH3·H2O, 25wt%) were received from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Company, China. Quinine sulfate (suitable for fluorescence) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company, China. Dialysis bag (1000Da, molecular weight cutoff) was supplied by Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company, China. All reagents used in this work were of analytical reagent grade, and used without further purification. Deionized water was used for aqueous solution preparation in all of the experiments.

The NCDs were prepared by hydrothermal method, employing citric acid as carbon source and ammonia solution as nitrogen source. A typical scheme is as follows: 4.2g of citric acid was dissolved in 40mL of deionized water and then mixed with 0.06mol (4.5mL) of ammonia solution under vigorous stirring to form a transparent solution. Then aliquots of the solution was transferred to 100mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves and heated at 120, 140, 160, 180, 200 and 220°C for 8h, separately. Subsequently, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. Then, the obtained crude product was filtered with 0.22μm anopore inorganic filter membranes to remove large particles and then extensively dialyzed over deionized water in a dialysis bag (with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000Da) with stirring and recharging with deionized water every 8h for 24h to eliminate any residual impurities. Finally, the as received pure NCD solution [black-blue (140 and 160°C), green (180°C) or light brown (200–220°C) (Table 1 )] was frozen in −86°C freezer for 20min and then freeze-dried for 24h to obtain black-blue (140 and 160°C), black-green (180°C) and reddish brown (200–220°C) NCD solid, which was then stored at room temperature in a desiccator owing to its moderate hygroscopic nature (Table 1). The NCDs prepared at 120, 140, 160, 180, 200 and 220°C are denoted as NCDs-120, NCDs-140, NCDs-160, NCDs-180, NCDs-200 and NCDs-220, respectively. In addition, under the same experiment condition as NCDs-180, the nitrogen-free CDs (N-free CDs-180) were also synthesized at 180°C by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid only, the obtained N-free CDs-180 crude product is pale yellow dispersion, after dialysis, pure N-free CDs-180 colourless solution was freeze-dried to obtain white solid (Table 1).

All the characterizations were performed at room temperature. PL spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of the CD solutions were conducted on a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluoromax-4 fluorophotometer with a 450W Xenon source and a Hitachi U3900 spectrophotometer, respectively. CD dilute solution in water was placed in quartz cuvettes (1.0cm pathlength). Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves were attained on an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrofluorometer using 375nm laser as the excitation source. HRTEM images were recorded with a JEOL JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope at operating voltage of 200kV. XRD patterns were recorded on a Rigaku-D/MAX 2500 diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation λ=0.15405nm) operating at 45kV and 200mA. FTIR spectra of CDs were recorded as KBr pellets with a BRUKER TENSOR27 spectrophotometer over the range of 4000–400cm−1. XPS investigation was performed using a Kratos AXIS Ultra spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. The data was analyzed by XPS PEAK4.1 software. Elemental analysis (C, H and/or N) was achieved using a Perkin Elmer 2400 analyzer.

The QY of all CD samples was determined by slope method. Quinine sulfate (literature QY=0.54 at 360nm) in 0.1M H2SO4 (n=1.33) was chosen as the standard reference sample to calculate the QY of CDs in water (n=1.33). All the absorbance values (keeping less than 0.1 at 360nm in order to minimize reabsorption) of the solutions were recorded on UV–vis spectrophotometer. PL spectra (excited at 360nm) of all sample solutions were measured on spectrometer. Integrated fluorescence intensity is the area under the PL curve in the wavelength range from 370 to 700nm. Then, a graph was plotted using the integrated fluorescence intensity against the absorbance and a trend line was added for each curve with intercept at zero. Then QY of CDs is calculated according to the Eq. (1):
(1) Qx =Qst (Gradx/Gradst)(ηx/ηst)2
where Q is the QY, Grad is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “st” refers to the quinine sulfate and “x” refers to CDs. For these aqueous solutions, ηx/ηst =1.

FTIR spectroscopy was then applied to further characterize the properties of chemical bonds and functional groups present on CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 , all NCDs contain the same groups as follows: the band at 3425cm−1 is assigned to OH stretching vibration [3,5,8,13–15] and that at 1404cm−1 corresponds to the deformation vibration of OH [14]. The band at 3188cm−1 is ascribed to NH stretching vibration [9,16], indicating N atoms have been doped into NCD framework. The bands at 2927cm−1 and 2860cm−1 are ascribed to stretching vibration of CH [3,8]. In addition, the band at 1575cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of CN [17], becomes weaker and band at 1683cm−1, which is ascribed to CO stretching vibration [18–20], becomes stronger with the increase of reaction temperature from 140°C to 220°C, which shows low-temperature (140–160°C) samples possess more amide groups [2,5]. Above results indicate that there exist abundant nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl groups, amino and amide groups on the surface of these NCDs. These hydrophilic functional groups impart NCDs excellent water solubility. For NCDs-180, the bands at 1726 and 1631cm−1 disappear, which are assigned to stretching vibration of carboxyl and carbonyl, respectively [3,14,17,18,21], and the band at 1217cm−1 becomes weaker, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of COH [14], suggesting the COOH groups of N-free CDs-180 are consumed owing to the dehydrolysis [2]. Band at 1726cm−1 which is ascribed to CO stretching vibration shifts to 1683cm−1 owing to nitrogen doping [18–20]. Two newly emerging bands at 3188cm−1 and 1575cm−1 are ascribed to NH and CN stretching vibration, respectively [9,16,17], indicating N atoms have doped into NCDs framework, and there exist amide and amine groups on the surface of NCDs. Above results indicate that there not only exist oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of NCDs-180, which is similar to that of N-free CDs-180 [22,23], but also exist nitrogen-containing functional groups such as amino and amide groups on the surface of NCDs-180. Nitrogen-containing functional groups impart NCDs-180 higher solubility in water.

XPS technique was used to further characterize the properties of chemical bonds and functional groups on the surface of NCDs and N-free CDs-180. NCDs-140 was not subject to XPS measurement owing to its high stickiness. In full scan XPS spectra of NCDs (Fig. 4 a), C1s, O1s and N1s signals are around 283, 530 and 399eV, respectively. XPS results indicate that the surface of NCDs is mainly composed of C, O and N elements [2,24]. Observed N peak confirms that these NCDs are doped with N. In addition, the C peak intensity turns stronger while O peak intensity turns weaker with elevating reaction temperature. Carbon to oxygen atomic ratio (RC/O) is calculated from full survey XPS spectra (Fig. 4a). The RC/O increases from 0.60 to 1.27, which indicates that carbonization degree of NCDs increases with the elevation of the reaction temperature. Whereas the N peak intensity keeps almost unchanged with increasing reaction temperature, indicating that elevating reaction temperature does not increase nitrogen doping degree significantly. For N-free CDs-180 (Fig. 4a), there exist only C and O elements [2], and no N peak is observed, confirming N-free CDs-180 does not contain N element. In curve-fitted C1s high resolution XPS spectra of NCDs, three peaks centre at 284.7–284.9, 286.0–286.4 and 288.3–288.6eV indicate the presence of CC/CC [25], CN/CO [2,18,26], and CO/CN bonds [2,12,18,23,27], respectively (Fig. 4b). For N-free CDs-180, the deconvoluted C1s XPS spectra show peaks at around 284.7, 286.4 and 288.8eV for CC/CC [2,18,25], CO [2,18], CO [2,18,27], respectively (Fig. 4b). This means the two kinds of CDs contain same C- and O-containing functional groups. The high-resolution O1s spectra of NCDs are shown in Fig. 4c, in which the deconvoluted peaks around 531.3–531.5 and 532.3–532.7eV can be assigned to CO and COH/COC groups, respectively [25,28]. Interestingly, the O1s XPS spectra of N-free CDs-180 can be decomposed into two peaks at 531.7 and 532.4eV, also indicating the presence of CO and COH/COC groups (Fig. 4c). Therefore, the O1s XPS fitting results are similar for the two kinds of CDs, they both contain similar O-containing functional groups, which enhance the solubility of the CDs in water [29]. The high resolution N1s XPS spectra can be decomposed into two peaks around 399.8–400.1 and 401–401.5eV, which are attributed to CNC and NH groups, respectively (Fig. 4d) [2,9,13,19,23,25,30,31]. The XPS analyses clearly demonstrate that N atoms have been doped into the framework of NCDs and some functional groups in pristine N-free CDs-180 like carboxyl and hydroxyl groups are also well restored. As shown in Table 2 , the elemental analysis results of NCDs also demonstrate the same change trend as XPS analysis that C content increases from 29.97% to 44.30% with an associated O content decreases from 49.89% to 34.80% while N content (in the range of 13.95%–15.14%) and H content (in the range of 6.195%–6.733%) keep almost unchanged with elevating reaction temperature. Compared with NCDs-180, N-free CDs-180 shows similar C content, indicating two kinds of CDs own the same carbonization degree under the same reaction temperature. In addition, N-free CDs-180 shows higher oxygen content with no N element, indicating N-free CDs-180 is carbon- and oxygen-rich nanodots. XPS and FTIR spectra, along with elemental analysis, confirm that these NCDs, containing elements nitrogen, carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen, own similar functional groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl/carbonyl, amide and amine moieties on particle surface. While N-free CDs-180, only containing the elements carbon, oxygen and hydrogen, are carbon-rich nanodots functionalized with hydroxyl, carboxyl/carbonyl moieties.

Fig. 6 presents the PL emission spectra of the NCDs-120, NCDs-140, NCDs-160, NCDs-180, NCDs-200, NCDs-220 and N-free CDs-180 aqueous solution under variable excitation wavelengths. The emission evolution of NCDs synthesized in the range of 120–180°C with excitation wavelength can be separated into two regions (Fig. 6a–d). Above 380nm, the obtained NCDs show the excitation-dependent emission behavior, the maximum emission peak red-shifts from 440 to 556nm, accompanied by the gradual reduction in peak intensity as the excitation wavelength increases from 380 to 500nm. On the contrary, below 380nm excitation, the emission peak basically centers at 440nm and peak intensity first increases, reaches a maximum at the excitation wavelength of 360nm, and then gradually decreases as the excitation wavelengths varies from 360nm to 380nm (Fig. 6a–d). However, the emission spectra of both NCDs-200 and NCDs-220 are substantially dependent on excitation wavelength (Fig. 6e and f). When excitation wavelength changes gradually from 300 to 500nm, the corresponding maximum emission peak red-shifts from 410 to 545nm and the peak intensity first increases, reaches a maximum under excitation wavelength of 340nm along with maximum emission wavelength of 426nm for NCDs-200 and 422nm for NCDs-220, respectively, and then decreases. Compared with the PL spectra of NCDs-180 aqueous solution under different excitation wavelengths, the PL spectra of N-free CDs-180 aqueous solution are seen to be excitation-dependent, the emission peak red-shifts from 425 to 543nm at excitations from 300 to 500nm, and the peak intensity first increases, reaches a maximum at the excitation wavelength of 360nm, and then gradually decreases with further increase in excitation wavelength. The excitation-dependent emission wavelength and intensity are common phenomena observed in carbon-based fluorescent materials [9]. Such excitation-dependent PL behavior is related to the different surface states of the CDs. There are multiple C- and O-containing functional groups on the surface of the produced CDs, resulting in various surface states with different energy levels and thus a series of emissive traps. Under different excitation wavelength, the corresponding surface state emissive trap is dominant, giving excitation-dependent PL [11,27,29,33,34]. This phenomenon allows for multiple colors emitted via PL under different excitation wavelengths, a property that is important for bioimaging and biomedical applications, which can avoid harmful UV or blue excitations [9]. However, the excitation-independent emission of NCDs implies uniform surface state emissive traps, which can avoid autofluorescence during their applications [31].

The QY of the CDs varies with the fabrication method and the surface chemistry involved [9]. The QY of CDs was determined by slope method, using quinine sulfate as a standard, excitation wavelength sets at 360nm, other more detailed information is shown in part 2.4. Fig. 7 and Table 3 show the QY results for NCDs prepared at 120, 140, 160, 180, 200 and 220°C and N-free CDs-180. QY of NCDs first increases in the range of 120–180°C, reaches a maximum at 180°C, and then gradually decreases in the range of 180–220°C, and the highest QY of NCDs-180 is calculated to be 35.4%, which is slightly lower than that of our previous NCDs prepared with citric acid and urea [8]. Compared with the QY (2.84%) of the N-free CDs-180, NCDs-180 show higher QY, indicating that doping N can really improve the QY of CDs [8].

Fig. 8 presents time-resolved PL curves of NCDs prepared at 140, 160, 180, 200, 220°C and N-free CDs-180 with excitation and emission wavelengths of 375 and 440nm at room temperature by using a time-correlated single photon counting technique. Results show that the five NCD samples exhibit similar spectral profiles (Fig. 8), and N-free CDs-180 and NCDs-180 also exhibit similar spectral profiles. Moreover, all the PL decay curves of CDs are best fitted to a biexponential function (2) [35,36]:
(2)
R ( t ) =
B 1
exp ( −
t /
τ 1
) +
B 2
exp ( −
t /
τ 2
)
where R(t) is the intensity usually assumed to decay as the sum of individual single exponential decays, B1 and B2 are the pre-exponential factors, τ1 and τ2 are the decay times. The average life time τave of CDs is calculated by Eq. (3) [35,36]:
(3)
τ
a v e
=
B 1
τ 1 2
+
B 2
τ 2 2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2

The best-fit results are listed in Table 4 . All NCDs prepared from 140 to 220°C contain a fast component (τ1 slightly increases from 1.34ns to 2.17ns) and a slow component (τ2 slightly increases from 6.61ns to 8.18ns), whereas τave slightly decreases from 6.610ns to 6.097ns. It is clear that all the NCDs show a comparable τave, indicating that reaction temperature has slight effect on fluorescence lifetime of NCDs. The τave of N-free CDs-180 is calculated to be 5.200ns, consisting of a short lifetime component of τ1 =1.67ns (22.66%) and a long lifetime component of τ2 =6.55ns (77.34%). It is clear that NCDs-180 has a longer τave than N-free CDs-180 (Table 4), which is consistent with Mao's work [37]. The fact that nitrogen atoms induce longer average lifetime may be due to suppression of non-radiative energy transitions in NCDs [32]. In addition, the τave of all CDs being in the nanosecond regime suggests that the synthesized CDs are very suitable for optoelectronic and biological applications [9].

Our experiment results show QY of NCDs first increases then decreases with the increase of reaction temperature (Fig. 7 and Table 3), whereas Giannelis [6,38] group and Yang [1,3,6] group show that QY of CDs gradually decreases with the increase of reaction temperature. Moreover, it is believed that QY of CDs is due to the number of molecular fluorophores, more molecular fluorophores lead to higher QY of CDs. In other words, at low temperatures pyrolysis results in large number of molecular fluorophores without carbon core, which leads to higher QY. As the pyrolysis proceeds to higher temperatures, part molecular fluorophores are gradually consumed to form carbon core, so the number of molecular fluorophores decreases, leading to reduced QY. Because QY changing law of CDs with reaction temperature is different from that of previous reports [1,3,6,38], the formation mechanism of NCDs prepared from low temperature to high temperature is schematically explained and divided into three steps (Scheme 1 ). The formation mechanism of NCDs is related with reaction temperature and QY.
(1)
Formation of polymer-like NCDs: when temperature increases from 120 to 160°C, polymer-like NCDs gradually form along with the increase in QY (Fig. 7 and Table 3). Both citric acid and ammonia are non-fluorescent small molecules. Under low hydrothermal temperature, the carboxyl and hydroxyl group of citric acid and amino group of ammonia proceed complex condensation reaction without carbonization, including intermolecular condensation between citric acid molecules themselves and between citric acid and ammonia molecules, to remove water molecules to form small fluorescent molecules [1,6,38]. With the increase of temperature, condensation degree gradually increases, small fluorescent molecules gradually grow up to form short fluorescent polymer chains (FPC). With the further increase of temperature, condensation degree further increases, the number of short FPC increases and short FPC gradually extends to form long FPC. Moreover, in the course of extension of short FPC, the chains further crosslink with each other to remove small molecules and to form larger polymer particles, which is named as polymer-like NCDs [3] with enhanced fluorescence (QY increases from 8.03% for NCDs-120 to 27.6% for NCDs-160). They own more polymer’s properties. Because short FPC gradually extends and increases in number, QY gradually increases. The large particles observed in TEM images may be the aggregation of FPC (short or long FPC) (Fig. 1a and b), such polymer-like NCDs contain no carbon core, O content is higher than C content (Table 2), and their solid state is sticky, as seen from the photoes (Table 1). Above results further verify the polymer-like NCDs have a certain degree of polymerization, while the degree of polymerization has not yet reached the degree needed to form carbon core.
(2)
Formation of carbon core: at higher temperatures from 160°C to 180°C, polymer-like NCDs gradually carbonize (Table 2) to form NCDs with carbon core (carbogenic NCDs), with increased QY (Fig. 7 and Table 3). At this stage, on one hand, short FPC continues to extend and increase in number, on the other hand, part FPC is consumed to build the carbon core (Fig. 1c). However, because the extension and number increasing rate of FPC is faster than its consumption rate for building the carbon core, the number of FPC on the surface of NCDs-180 increases compared with that of NCDs-160, which leads to increase in QY.
(3)
Growing up of carbon core: at even higher temperatures from 180°C to 220°C, carbon core gradually grows up (Fig. 1d and e) and the number of FPC on the surface of NCDs decreases, both leading to the decrease in QY of NCDs (Fig. 7 and Table 3). Owing to the further condensation and carbonization (Table 2) to remove small molecules with the increase of temperature, more FPC on NCDs surface is consumed to further grow the carbon core. The extension and number increasing rate of FPC is slower than the consumption rate of FPC, in other words, the number of FPC on NCDs surface decreases, which leads to the decrease in QY. Therefore NCDs-180 shows highest QY.

In short, QY of NCDs depends largely on the number of FPC, the competition between FPC formation on the surface of NCDs and carbon core growth leads to the change in number of FPC, and consequently to the NCDs with highest QY at appropriate hydrothermal temperature. At low temperature (120≤T≤160°C), polymer-like NCDs are obtained, and QY increases owing to the continuous formation of short FPC and the growth of short FPC into long FPC. At moderate temperature (160<T≤180°C), polymer-like NCDs are gradually carbonized to form carbon core-containing NCDs and QY increases owing to the increase of FPC, as a result of faster extension and formation FPC than its consumption. At high temperature (180<T≤220°C), carbon core gradually grows up and QY decreases owing to the decrease in FPC as a result of slower extension and formation of FPC than its consumption.

In summary, 6 kinds of NCDs prepared at different temperatures and N-free CDs-180 were synthesized. Results show that all NCDs exhibit bright blue fluorescence. All NCDs contain similar functional groups, nitrogen doping degree and fluorescence lifetime. With the increase of reaction temperature, carbonization degree increases, while QY, UV–vis absorbance and PL intensity first increase and then decrease. Compared with N-free CDs, NCDs possess enchanced QY and longer fluorescence lifetime, and the nitrogen-containing functional groups on their surface influence the UV–vis absorbance and PL spectra. The formation mechanism of NCDs from polymer-like NCDs to carbogenic NCDs is proposed: QY of NCDs depends largely on the number of FPC. On one hand, with reaction temperature increasing, the formation rate of FPC is faster than its consumption rate for carbon core growth, the number of FPC increases, which leads to increase in QY. On the other hand, carbon core gradually grows up from the consumption of FPC, the formation rate of FPC is slower than its consumption rate, the number of FPC decreases, leading to decreased QY; the competition result of above two factors is QY of NCDs reaches maximum at appropriate temperature.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21176169), International Science & Technology Cooperation Program of China (Grant No. 2012DFR50460), Shanxi Provincial Key Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology (Grant No. 2015013002-10) and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (Grant No. 2012-038) for finacial support.
